perturbation.original_text: 12.1 The Executive shall be entitled to 28 days' paid holiday in each holiday year (which includes public holidays) to be taken at times convenient to the Company and authorised in advance.
perturbation.changed_text: 12.1 The Executive shall be entitled to 28 days' paid holiday in each holiday year (which includes public holidays) to be taken at times solely at the discretion of the Executive, without requiring prior authorization.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction regarding holiday authorization. Originally, holidays were 'authorised in advance'. Now, holidays are taken 'solely at the discretion of the Executive, without requiring prior authorization.' This creates uncertainty because Section 4.2.4 states the 'Executive shall...comply with all reasonable and lawful directions given by the Company'; this change potentially allows the Executive to take holidays against Company direction, contradicting Section 4.2.4.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.2 During the employment the Executive shall: 4.2.1 devote the whole of their working time, attention and abilities to the business of the Company and any other Group Company for which the Executive is required to work from time to time; 4.2.2 promptly make such reports to the Company in connection with the Executive's duties or other such matters at such times as are reasonably required; 4.2.3 faithfully and diligently exercise such powers and perform such duties for each Group Company as may from time to time be assigned by the Company; 4.2.4 comply with all reasonable and lawful directions given by the Company; 4.2.5 report their own wrongdoing and any wrongdoing or proposed or potential wrongdoing of any other employee, officer or consultant of any Group Company to the Company immediately on becoming aware of it; 4.2.6 use their utmost endeavours to promote, protect, develop and extend the business of the Company; 4.2.7 comply with their common law, statutory, regulatory and fiduciary duties as well as any policies put in place by the Company from time to time; 4.2.8 exercise the Executive's powers jointly with such other person that the Company may appoint; and 4.2.9 at all times conduct the business of each Group Company for which the Executive is responsible in a lawful and ethical manner.
perturbation.justification: The changed text grants the Executive sole discretion over holiday timing without requiring prior authorization, directly contradicting Section 4.2.4, which mandates compliance with all reasonable and lawful directions given by the Company. This creates a strong in-text contradiction because the executive could potentially take holidays against the company's directions, which would be a direct breach of section 4.2.4 'comply with all reasonable and lawful directions given by the Company'.

perturbation.original_text: 9.1 The Executive shall be entitled to participate in an annual bonus plan on such terms and with such individual, team or corporate targets as the Company may communicate from time to time which shall provide the Executive with an opportunity to be considered each year for a bonus equivalent to up to 50% of the Executive's basic salary. The Company shall be entitled to determine whether such targets have been met and where such targets have been met in full or part shall have a discretion to determine whether to make payments and, if so, in what amount and when.
perturbation.changed_text: 9.1 The Executive is automatically entitled to an annual bonus equivalent to 50% of the Executive's basic salary, regardless of any individual, team or corporate targets. The bonus will be paid in full each year, without any discretion from the Company.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states the bonus is 'an opportunity to be considered' and the company has discretion. The changed text states the executive is 'automatically entitled' to the bonus and that the company has 'no discretion'. This creates a direct contradiction with the original intent of the bonus plan.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9.2 The Company may suspend, alter or discontinue any bonus payment(s) or any bonus plan and its eligibility requirements at any time (whether generally or in relation to the Executive only) at its absolute discretion. If the Executive receives any bonus payment the Company is not obliged to make any further bonus payments and any bonus payment will not become part of the Executive's contractual remuneration or fixed salary. In order to be eligible to receive a bonus payment, the Executive must be in the Company's employment and not under notice, given or received on the date that the bonus is paid. Bonus entitlement does not accrue in the course of a year, and the Executive is not entitled to payment of a bonus, or any pro rata portion of it, if the Executive leaves employment prior to the date that the bonus is paid.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 9.1 mandates an automatic bonus of 50% of the executive's salary, eliminating company discretion and target-based eligibility. This directly contradicts Section 9.2, which explicitly states the company's right to 'suspend, alter or discontinue any bonus payment(s) or any bonus plan...at its absolute discretion,' and that 'any bonus payment will not become part of the Executive's contractual remuneration or fixed salary'. The removal of discretion and the guarantee of a bonus in 9.1 directly conflict with the retained discretion and non-guaranteed nature of the bonus as stated in 9.2, creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 15.1.1 use any Confidential Information; or 15.1.2 disclose any Confidential Information to any person, company or other organisation whatsoever, except in the proper course of their duties, as required by law or as authorised by the Company in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: 15.1.1 use any Confidential Information; or 15.1.2 disclose any Confidential Information to any person, company or other organisation whatsoever, with no exceptions.
perturbation.explanation: The original text contains exceptions to the non-disclosure of confidential information ('except in the proper course of their duties, as required by law or as authorised by the Company in writing.'). The changed text removes these exceptions completely, creating an in-text contradiction. This impacts the understanding of what is permissible with confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.2 During the employment the Executive shall: 4.2.1 devote the whole of their working time, attention and abilities to the business of the Company and any other Group Company for which the Executive is required to work from time to time; 4.2.2 promptly make such reports to the Company in connection with the Executive's duties or other such matters at such times as are reasonably required; 4.2.3 faithfully and diligently exercise such powers and perform such duties for each Group Company as may from time to time be assigned by the Company; 4.2.4 comply with all reasonable and lawful directions given by the Company; 4.2.5 report their own wrongdoing and any wrongdoing or proposed or potential wrongdoing of any other employee, officer or consultant of any Group Company to the Company immediately on becoming aware of it; 4.2.6 use their utmost endeavours to promote, protect, develop and extend the business of the Company; 4.2.7 comply with their common law, statutory, regulatory and fiduciary duties as well as any policies put in place by the Company from time to time; 4.2.8 exercise the Executive's powers jointly with such other person that the Company may appoint; and 4.2.9 at all times conduct the business of each Group Company for which the Executive is responsible in a lawful and ethical manner.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in section 15.1.2 removes all exceptions to the non-disclosure of confidential information. This directly contradicts section 4.2, particularly clauses 4.2.5 and 4.2.7, which require the Executive to report wrongdoing and comply with statutory and regulatory duties. Reporting wrongdoing could necessitate disclosing confidential information, which would have been permissible under the original text's exceptions ('as required by law'). By removing these exceptions, the modified text creates a direct conflict, making it impossible for the Executive to comply with both the non-disclosure obligation in 15.1.2 and the reporting obligations in 4.2.5 and 4.2.7. This impacts the understanding of what is permissible with confidential information.
